draft.title: weather app for runners
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
weather app for runners

Building your first draft…

facade.hypothesis: Runners prioritize real-time pace feedback over ambient weather context
facade.label: Mile splits in big numbers
facade.acceptImplies: User wants glanceable, quantitative performance data dominating the screen
facade.rejectImplies: User wants weather as primary story, with running stats nested or secondary

evidence.hypothesis: Runners prioritize real-time pace feedback over ambient weather context
evidence.implication: User wants glanceable, quantitative performance data dominating the screen

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from high contrast, light backgrounds, and card-based layout that scans quickly during outdoor use
facade.label: Bright, minimal cards
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers clean, airy interfaces with clear visual separation—likely values readability on sunny runs and modern aesthetic
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer darker themes for eye comfort, denser information density, or a more atmospheric/immersive design language

facade.hypothesis: A weather app for runners benefits from friendly, motivational language that builds confidence rather than neutral meteorological reporting
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and personality: friendly vs professional
facade.label: Warm, encouraging tone
facade.acceptImplies: Users prefer conversational, supportive copy that feels like a running coach or friend (e.g., 'Perfect pace weather!' instead of '72°F, 3mph wind')
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer factual, clean delivery without personality—focus on data clarity and minimalist copy (e.g., just the conditions without commentary)

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
Whether user wants calm, reassuring interface vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
Whether user prefers smooth, flowing animations vs. snappy, instant feedback

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimalist, clean aesthetics vs. data-rich visual displays for workout tracking
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants quick-glance info at top vs. scrollable detailed forecast layers
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational, encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, reassuring interface vs. energetic, action-oriented feel
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes running-specific metrics (pace impact, humidity) vs. standard weather only
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers smooth, flowing animations vs. snappy, instant feedback
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: For a runner, motion synced to biometric data (heart rate) creates emotional connection and trust in real-time data, vs. generic smooth animation.
facade.label: Pace pulses with heartbeat
facade.acceptImplies: Runner expects feedback loops tied to *their* body, not the interface's timing. Prefer visceral, bio-linked motion cues.
facade.rejectImplies: Runner wants clean, predictable interface motion independent of physiology. Distrust or overstimulation from too-close body sync.

facade.hypothesis: Runners want visual energy and motivation in their app interface — vibrant colors and dynamic contrast that match the intensity of their workout.
facade.label: Bright, energetic colors
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use bold, saturated colors (bright blues, oranges, reds) and high-contrast UI to create momentum and excitement, reflecting running's intensity.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer a subdued, muted, or monochromatic palette that feels grounding and meditative — visual restraint rather than stimulation.

facade.hypothesis: Runners want weather conditions as a secondary glance element, not the primary focus
facade.axisTargeted: Information hierarchy — performance metrics vs environmental context
facade.label: Weather bar at top
facade.acceptImplies: Weather should be condensed (icon + temp) above the fold, letting pace data dominate
facade.rejectImplies: Weather needs equal or greater screen real estate than split times and pace metrics

facade.hypothesis: Runners prioritize real-time metrics (pace, heart rate, distance) in a persistent left column, minimizing scroll friction during runs
facade.axisTargeted: Navigation density & information hierarchy — persistent sidebar vs full-screen scrolling
facade.label: Sidebar with live stats
facade.acceptImplies: User values glanceable, always-visible key metrics; design should anchor stats left, content right; information architecture favors depth over breadth
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers immersive full-screen content or tap-to-reveal metrics; design should use overlays, bottom sheets, or modal reveals; sidebar feels cramped or distracting

draft.title: RunWeather
draft.summary: A weather app designed for runners, showing real-time conditions, wind impact, and run-friendly forecasts at a glance.
draft.html:

Good
